title: Public Health Agency of Canada's Regulatory Stock Review Plan: 2026 to 2029 - Canada.ca
content:
## Public Health Agency of Canada's Regulatory Stock Review Plan: 2026 to 2029
The Regulatory Stock Review Plan is a public list and description of reviews of regulations that the Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) plans to undertake within a 3-year period.
The stock review plan is developed through careful consideration and consultation on PHAC regulations to determine whether these continue to meet their objectives and how they can better serve people in Canada. It is intended to give people in Canada, including businesses, Indigenous Peoples, and trading partners an opportunity to inform the reviews of regulations and to plan for the future.
The stock review plan will be adjusted and updated every year to reflect PHAC's regulatory priorities and changes to the operating environment.

### Amendments to the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations
Amendments to the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations
Human Pathogens and Toxins Act
As part of the Regulatory Impact Analysis Statement, a commitment was made to adhere to the Government's life-cycle approach to regulatory development through evaluation of the effectiveness of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations (HPTR) in meeting its objectives. In 2021, PHAC's Office of Audit and Evaluation (OAE) completed the first evaluation on the effectiveness and efficiency of activities related to the implementation of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act and Regulations (HPTA/R). The evaluation focused on the effectiveness and efficiency of the regulatory framework from 2015/16 and 2020/21 and included the Centre for Biosecurity's Management Response and Action Plan (MRAP). It identified areas for regulatory amendments including enhancements to the security clearance program and opportunities to increase clarity and decrease administrative burden on parties regulated by both PHAC and CFIA. Proposed regulatory amendments will also respond to the evaluation recommendations as applicable. In 2026, as part of PHAC’s commitment to evidence-based regulatory oversight and continuous improvement, the Centre for Biosecurity  underwent a Biosecurity Program evaluation led by OAE. The evaluation included consideration of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations, built on the previous evaluation of the HPTA/R, and examined progress against the related (MRAP), the effectiveness of the current regulatory framework, and ongoing efforts to modernize biosecurity oversight. Covering fiscal years 2019–2020 to 2024–2025, the evaluation drew on multiple lines of evidence, including performance and financial information and input from internal and external stakeholders. The report is expected to be published in May 2027, and its findings will help inform PHAC’s ongoing assessment of the Regulations and any future changes, as appropriate.
As part of the Regulatory Impact Analysis Statement, a commitment was made to adhere to the Government's life-cycle approach to regulatory development through evaluation of the effectiveness of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations (HPTR) in meeting its objectives.
In 2021, PHAC's Office of Audit and Evaluation (OAE) completed the first evaluation on the effectiveness and efficiency of activities related to the implementation of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act and Regulations (HPTA/R). The evaluation focused on the effectiveness and efficiency of the regulatory framework from 2015/16 and 2020/21 and included the Centre for Biosecurity's Management Response and Action Plan (MRAP). It identified areas for regulatory amendments including enhancements to the security clearance program and opportunities to increase clarity and decrease administrative burden on parties regulated by both PHAC and CFIA. Proposed regulatory amendments will also respond to the evaluation recommendations as applicable.
In 2026, as part of PHAC’s commitment to evidence-based regulatory oversight and continuous improvement, the Centre for Biosecurity  underwent a Biosecurity Program evaluation led by OAE. The evaluation included consideration of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Regulations, built on the previous evaluation of the HPTA/R, and examined progress against the related (MRAP), the effectiveness of the current regulatory framework, and ongoing efforts to modernize biosecurity oversight. Covering fiscal years 2019–2020 to 2024–2025, the evaluation drew on multiple lines of evidence, including performance and financial information and input from internal and external stakeholders. The report is expected to be published in May 2027, and its findings will help inform PHAC’s ongoing assessment of the Regulations and any future changes, as appropriate.
Approach to the Review
This evaluation examined to what extent the Biosecurity Program has adapted to a changing environment, its progress towards achieving intended outcomes, and how data is leveraged to inform program activities. The evaluation focused on activities related to the implementation of the existing regulatory framework, as well as ongoing work to modernize the biosecurity oversight framework from fiscal years 2019-2020 to 2024-2025. It also followed up on the previous Evaluation of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act and Regulations Framework . To address the evaluation questions, information was collected from documents, performance information, literature, and financial data, as well as interviews with internal staff and external partners and stakeholders.

It also followed up on the previous Evaluation of the Human Pathogens and Toxins Act and Regulations Framework .
To address the evaluation questions, information was collected from documents, performance information, literature, and financial data, as well as interviews with internal staff and external partners and stakeholders.

## Reviews to be initiated within the next three years (2026-2029)

### Amending the Schedule to the Quarantine Act
Amending the Schedule to the Quarantine Act
Amending the Schedule to the Quarantine Act
The Quarantine Act includes a list of specific diseases that trigger public health measures. Currently, each disease is listed one by one. This makes it harder to respond quickly when new diseases appear or when risks change, because the law has to be updated each time. This can slow down action and create extra administrative burden.
The Quarantine Act includes a list of specific diseases that trigger public health measures. Currently, each disease is listed one by one. This makes it harder to respond quickly when new diseases appear or when risks change, because the law has to be updated each time. This can slow down action and create extra administrative burden.
Approach to the review
As part of the Government of Canada’s Red Tape Review, we will review the Schedule to the Quarantine Act to group individually listed diseases into broader, more flexible categories. This will make it easier to address health threats by reducing the need to update the law as often.
As part of the Government of Canada’s Red Tape Review, we will review the Schedule to the Quarantine Act to group individually listed diseases into broader, more flexible categories. This will make it easier to address health threats by reducing the need to update the law as often.
Date of last review or amendment
Last review in 2023. Last amendment in 2023.
Last review in 2023.
Last amendment in 2023.
Targeted start for review

### Repealing and Replacing the Quarantine Regulations
Repealing and Replacing the Quarantine Regulations
Repealing and Replacing the Quarantine Regulations
The Quarantine Act and Quarantine Regulations work together to help prevent the introduction and spread of communicable diseases in relation to travellers and conveyance operators arriving in, and in the process of departing Canada. Some reporting requirements in the Quarantine Regulations are outdated and do not match the current reporting approach in the Quarantine Act . The Public Health Agency of Canada plans to repeal and replace sections 12 and 19 of the Quarantine Regulations that duplicate reporting requirements found in section 34 of the Quarantine Act . Updating the Quarantine Regulations will clarify existing reporting requirements and make them easier to follow.
The Quarantine Act and Quarantine Regulations work together to help prevent the introduction and spread of communicable diseases in relation to travellers and conveyance operators arriving in, and in the process of departing Canada. Some reporting requirements in the Quarantine Regulations are outdated and do not match the current reporting approach in the Quarantine Act . The Public Health Agency of Canada plans to repeal and replace sections 12 and 19 of the Quarantine Regulations that duplicate reporting requirements found in section 34 of the Quarantine Act . Updating the Quarantine Regulations will clarify existing reporting requirements and make them easier to follow.
Approach to the Review
As part of the Government of Canada’s Red Tape Review, we will review the Quarantine Regulations to: identify reporting requirements that no longer apply due to changes to the Quarantine Act over the years identify opportunities to simplify language and improve clarity add further information on current requirements that apply to travellers and conveyance operators under the Quarantine Act
As part of the Government of Canada’s Red Tape Review, we will review the Quarantine Regulations to:
- identify reporting requirements that no longer apply due to changes to the Quarantine Act over the years
- identify opportunities to simplify language and improve clarity
- add further information on current requirements that apply to travellers and conveyance operators under the Quarantine Act
Date of last review or amendment
Last review in 2023. Last amended in 2006.
Last review in 2023.
Last amended in 2006.
Targeted start for review
